claim: During pregnancy, if the mother suffers organ damage, the baby in the womb sends stem cells to repair the damaged organ.

result.claim: During pregnancy, if the mother suffers organ damage, the baby in the womb sends stem cells to repair the damaged organ.
result.reasoning: While the search results confirm the phenomenon of fetal cell microchimerism, where fetal cells (including lymphoid progenitors) transfer to the mother and can persist for decades, and even differentiate into functional immune cells (Paper ID 16, propositions such as prop_122, prop_133, prop_132, prop_112, prop_120, prop_136, prop_137, prop_130, prop_125, prop_121, prop_114, prop_131, prop_129, prop_126, prop_123, prop_127, prop_128), there is no direct evidence in the provided search results to support the claim that these fetal stem cells actively *repair damaged maternal organs*. The papers discuss the presence and differentiation of these cells and their potential impact on maternal health, but do not explicitly state or demonstrate a reparative function for maternal organ damage. One chunk even mentions fetal T lymphocytes as a "possible cause for autoimmune diseases" (chunk_21), suggesting a potentially negative impact rather than a reparative one. Therefore, based solely on the provided evidence, there is insufficient information to verify the claim.

evidence.text: The resulting fetal cells are functional T lymphocytes in the periphery.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: NK cells (CD56/16+) of fetal origin have been detected in the circulation of a large proportion of healthy women that had children.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: The phenomenon of fetal T cells in the maternal thymus was observed in 12 of 68 tested mice.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Mothers acquire fetal lymphoid progenitors during gestation that develop into functional T cells.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: B lymphocytes (CD19+) of fetal origin have been detected in the circulation of a large proportion of healthy women that had children.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: CD19+IgM+ fetal B cells were found in maternal RAG−/− bone marrow and spleen after similar matings.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: T cells were found in the maternal spleen.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: CD34+CD38+ cells of fetal origin persist for decades in mothers after gestation.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal cell microchimerism persists in some women for decades after delivery.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal cells have been detected in maternal circulating CD4+ populations in 31% of tested women after pregnancy.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: The resulting fetal cells are functional B lymphocytes in the periphery.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal double-positive T cells (CD4+CD8+) were recovered in the maternal thymus during pregnancy.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: The phenomenon of fetal T cells in the maternal thymus was observed in 0 of 51 control mice.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Monocytes (CD14+) of fetal origin have been detected in the circulation of a large proportion of healthy women that had children.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal cells have been detected in maternal circulating CD3+ populations in 70% of tested women after pregnancy.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Lymphocytes of fetal origin in maternal circulation have been demonstrated in several studies.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal hemopoietic progenitor cells in maternal blood during pregnancy have been suspected functionally as colony-forming progenitors following in vitro culture of maternal blood.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal cell microchimerism has been reported in 30–70% of women with prior pregnancy history.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal cells enter the maternal circulation during pregnancy.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal cells have been detected in maternal circulating CD8+ populations in 64% of tested women after pregnancy.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers

evidence.text: Fetal hemopoietic progenitor cells in maternal blood during pregnancy have been suspected by revealing their cell surface CD34+ expression.
evidence.source: Pregnancy Allows the Transfer and Differentiation of Fetal Lymphoid Progenitors into Functional T and B Cells in Mothers
